content.problemStatement:
An array AAA consisting of NNN integers is called KKK-beautiful if it holds the following property:
The number of adjacent indices with different values is at most KKK. More formally, there are at most KKK indices iii (1≤i<N1\le i\lt N1≤i<N) such that Ai≠Ai+1A_i\ne A_{i+1}Ai​=Ai+1​. Note that according to this definition, if an array AAA is KKK-beautiful, then AAA is also (K+1)(K+1)(K+1)-beautiful.

You are given an array AAA consisting of NNN integers and an integer KKK. Find the length of the longest KKK-beautiful subsequence of AAA, where a subsequence is defined below.

For example, consider the array A=[1,1,2,3,2,4]A = [1 , 1, 2, 3, 2 , 4]A=[1,1,2,3,2,4]. The subsequences [1,1][1, 1][1,1] and [1,1,2][1, 1, 2][1,1,2] are 111-beautiful, and the subsequences [1,2,3][1, 2, 3][1,2,3] and [1,2,2,4][1, 2, 2, 4][1,2,2,4] are 222-beautiful.

**Note:** A sequence aaa is a subsequence of a sequence bbb if aaa can be obtained from bbb by deletion of several (possibly, zero) elements. For example, [3,1][3,1][3,1] is a subsequence of [3,2,1][3,2,1][3,2,1] and [4,3,1][4,3,1][4,3,1], but not a subsequence of [1,3,3,7][1,3,3,7][1,3,3,7] and [3,10,4][3,10,4][3,10,4].


Input Format
The first line contains an integer TTT - the number of test cases. Then TTT test cases follow.


The first line of each test case contains two integers NNN, KKK - the length of array AAA and the parameter for a beautiful sequence.


The second line of each testcase contains NNN integers A1,…,ANA_1,\ldots, A_NA1​,…,AN​.



Output Format
For each test case, output the maximum possible length of a KKK-beautiful subsequence of AAA.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with a line containing two integers N and K. The next line contains N integers A1 … AN, the elements of the array.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with the maximum possible length of a K‑beautiful subsequence of the given array.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1 : You can either choose [1,1,2][1, 1, 2][1,1,2] or [1,1,3][1, 1, 3][1,1,3] as a subsequence. Both of the subsequences are 111-beautiful. So the maximum length of a 111-beautiful subsequence is 333.


Test Case 2 : It is optimal to choose the subsequence [1,2,2,5,5][1, 2, 2, 5, 5][1,2,2,5,5]. So the maximum length of a 222-beautiful subsequence is 555.


Test Case 3 : You can choose the entire array as a subsequence because the given array is KKK-beautiful for any K≥0K\ge 0K≥0, which includes K=5K=5K=5.

solution.approach:
Let dp[t][v] be the maximum length of a subsequence that ends with value v and uses exactly t runs (t‑1 changes). While scanning the array from left to right we can extend a subsequence in two ways:
1. Take the current element a and continue a subsequence that already ends with a (same run). This adds 1 to dp[t][a].
2. Start a new run with a after any subsequence that used t‑1 runs and ends with a different value. The best length among all values for t‑1 runs is stored in best[t‑1]. Adding the current a gives best[t‑1] + 1.
For each element we update dp[t][a] for t = K+1 … 1 (descending to avoid using the just‑updated values). After each update we also refresh best[t] = max(best[t], dp[t][a]). The answer for a test case is max_{1≤t≤K+1} best[t].
The algorithm runs in O(N·K) time and O(K·V) memory, where V = 1000 is the maximum possible value of Ai.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(K·V) where V = 1001 (≈ 1e6 integers, well within limits).
